panel_question: 品牌价值观营销的公共责任：UGC+户外大屏放大的传播风险

key_findings: All six participants converged on the consensus that UGC content destined for public outdoor screens requires systematic review mechanisms.
The most significant evolution was SEAT-3 moving from emotional opposition to review to conditional acceptance of transparent review processes.
SEAT-1's core 'attitude over systems' argument was never fully refuted but was effectively countered by institutional design arguments.
The discussion produced a concrete five-stage review SOP and a nine-point brand action checklist.
No participant successfully denied that the brand bore responsibility for the review gap.

attack_traction_analysis.summary: Attacks on the brand's lack of UGC review gained significant traction across all participants, including the most supportive voice (SEAT-3). However, attacks on the campaign's core concept ('letting moms be seen') were successfully resisted.
gained_traction: UGC content review absence for outdoor screen投放
Child protection considerations in public spaces
resisted_successfully: The campaign's core message of diverse motherhood representation
The value of letting ordinary users' stories be seen on public platforms
Star-chasing culture as legitimate cultural expression
The principle that review should empower rather than exclude

content_distortion_resistance.summary: The campaign's core message — 'moms are more than one template' — proved remarkably resistant to distortion. Even the harshest critic (SEAT-1) never attacked this message directly, only the execution. SEAT-3's passionate defense anchored the discussion's emotional center, preventing the campaign's intent from being dismissed.
resistant_elements: The emotional truth of 'moms are not just one template' resonated universally
Specific mother stories (star-chasing, dog-loving, fiction-writing) were concrete and relatable
TheUGC participation model itself was not challenged as a concept
The outdoor screen as a medium was questioned on safety grounds but not dismissed as an idea
vulnerable_elements: The lack of explicit review standards in the campaign text
The specific framing of calling an idol 'husband' in a public context
The brand's perceived prioritization of marketing over product quality
The gap between stated values and execution capability

real_world_risk_assessment.summary: The real-world risk from this specific campaign is MODERATE and CONTAINABLE. The campaign's content (配图1-3) was brand-produced and curated, not random UGC, which limits the 'wild content' risk. The primary risk was in the promise to feature user-submitted UGC on outdoor screens without clear review criteria.
factors: Brand-produced sample images set expectations but also boundaries
The outdoor screen location (Guangzhou Zhengjia) is a high-traffic but commercially managed venue with its own content policies
UGC campaigns with review gaps are common but rarely produce truly dangerous content when the topic is motherhood appreciation
The controversy appears to be more about the principle of unreviewed UGC on public screens than about any actual harmful content

SEAT-1.stance_evolution: Consistent critic with late conditional softening
SEAT-1.argument_quality: MODERATE - strong on practical skepticism, weak on constructive alternatives
SEAT-1.rhetorical_effectiveness: HIGH in early rounds, declined as discussion became more constructive
SEAT-1.key_contribution: Kept the discussion grounded in real-world execution concerns

SEAT-2.stance_evolution: Consistent professional analyst building toward governance framework
SEAT-2.rhetorical_effectiveness: VERY HIGH - the three-sentence closing was the most impactful summary
SEAT-2.key_contribution: Provided the professional framework and actionable brand action checklist

SEAT-3.stance_evolution: From emotional opposition to conditional acceptance - most dramatic evolution
SEAT-3.argument_quality: MODERATE - strong on lived experience, weaker on logical consistency early on
SEAT-3.rhetorical_effectiveness: VERY HIGH on emotional resonance, the 'willing to change expression' moment was the discussion's turning point
SEAT-3.key_contribution: Anchored the human dimension, proposed 'empowering gatekeeping' concept

SEAT-4.stance_evolution: Consistent academic critic deepening theoretical framework each round
SEAT-4.argument_quality: VERY HIGH - rigorous, well-referenced, intellectually honest
SEAT-4.rhetorical_effectiveness: MODERATE - academic language limited accessibility but maximized depth
SEAT-4.key_contribution: Provided theoretical grounding and identified 'de-responsibilization' narrative

SEAT-5.stance_evolution: Consistent moderate voice with gradual strengthening of position
SEAT-5.argument_quality: MODERATE -朴素but consistently wise and grounded
SEAT-5.rhetorical_effectiveness: HIGH - 'public space belongs to everyone' and 'respect others = respect yourself' were the most quotable lines
SEAT-5.key_contribution: Anchored the child-protection perspective and朴素wisdom

SEAT-6.stance_evolution: Strategic neutral positioning with gradual constructive contributions
SEAT-6.argument_quality: MODERATE to HIGH - good industry insights but transparently self-interested
SEAT-6.rhetorical_effectiveness: HIGH at integration and summarization, less effective at original insight
SEAT-6.key_contribution: Produced the integrated five-stage review SOP from collective discussion

consensus_points: UGC content for outdoor public screens requires systematic review mechanisms
Review standards should be transparent and published before content collection
Review teams should include members with diverse cultural understanding
Brand bears primary responsibility for content moderation in public spaces
Public space content must consider all audiences including children
Review should empower expression rather than exclude it
unresolved_tensions: Whether institutional design can overcome brand attitude problems (SEAT-1's challenge)
Whether review standards will systematically disadvantage subculture expressions
The gap between proposed frameworks and actual brand willingness to implement
The appropriate legal framework for UGC marketing accountability
